pages.epigraph: “Motherhood changes your routine. It does not reduce your potential.”

blocks.text: If someone asked you to introduce yourself today, would the words “I used to…” appear somewhere in the answer? I used to work. I used to study. I used to feel confident.

Motherhood can make a life beautifully full while quietly moving parts of your identity to the edge. Careers pause, plans change and paid achievements become less visible. That does not mean your contribution or ability disappeared.
The skills you are using now

rows: Planning meals, appointments and school routines

blocks.text: These skills do not replace professional knowledge. They prove that you can learn and manage complex things. Financial confidence begins when you stop treating yourself as a beginner with nothing to offer.

blocks.label: YOUR CHOICE MATTERS
blocks.text: This book will not push you toward paid work or away from home. It will help make sure that whichever path you choose is supported by knowledge rather than fear.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: A career break is not an ability break. You are beginning with experience, resilience and the capacity to learn.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Write three strengths motherhood has added to your life and one skill you would enjoy learning next.

pages.epigraph: “Security is not only a number. It is the ability to understand, prepare and choose.”

blocks.text: Ask ten mothers what financial security means and you may hear ten answers: paid bills, an emergency fund, no debt, enough insurance, a return-to-work plan or simply knowing where the important documents are.
Financial confidence has four practical parts: visibility, protection, participation and options.

rows: You know the broad income, expenses, debts, accounts and goals.
The family has emergency money, suitable insurance and organised documents.
You can ask questions and take part in important decisions.
You know that earning, learning and financial choices remain available if life changes.

blocks.text: A large income can exist without confidence. A modest household can feel calm when money has a plan. The aim is not to control every rupee or know every product. It is to reduce avoidable uncertainty.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Confidence is not knowing all the answers. It is knowing where the facts are and what question to ask next.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Finish this sentence: “I would feel more financially confident if I understood…”

pages.chapterTitle: Money Is a Tool, Not a Test
pages.epigraph: “You do not have to be naturally good with money. Money management is a learnable skill.”

blocks.text: People say “I am terrible with money” as if finance were a personality trait. Yet few of us were formally taught how to read a bank statement, compare insurance, understand tax or invest.
Money is a tool. It can buy food, medicine, education, convenience, time, choices and peace of mind. Its purpose is to support the life you value—not measure your worth.

pages.chapterTitle: Money Is a Tool, Not a Test

blocks.text: Four basic jobs

rows: What does our life need or value today?
What will we need soon or unexpectedly?
Which long-term goals need help from investing?
What people or causes do we choose to support?

pages.chapterTitle: Money Is a Tool, Not a Test

blocks.label: A USEFUL PAUSE
blocks.text: Before a purchase or investment, ask: “What job is this money doing?” The answer is often more useful than “Is this good or bad?”

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Money is not the destination. The life and choices it supports are.

pages.chapterTitle: Money Is a Tool, Not a Test

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one recent expense and write what it really bought: necessity, time, convenience, joy, learning or peace of mind.

pages.epigraph: “Awareness is not judgement. It is the beginning of choice.”

blocks.text: A family can earn well and still reach the end of each month wondering where the money went. Often it is not one dramatic purchase. It is a collection of ordinary decisions, renewals, deliveries, fees and subscriptions.
Track without blame

For one week, record every outflow. Do not change anything yet. Group it into essentials, commitments, flexible spending, goals and unnoticed leakage. A budget built on shame rarely lasts; a budget built on facts can adapt.

rows: What is fixed and until when?
Are we paying the future too?
What can be cancelled or corrected?

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: You do not need to control every rupee. You need enough visibility to direct the next one.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Track spending for seven days using a notebook or phone note. Observe first; edit later.

pages.chapterTitle: Looking at Your Money Without Fear
pages.epigraph: “You do not have to fix everything today. You only have to be willing to look.”

blocks.text: Money can become like a drawer we avoid opening because we are afraid of what we may find. Avoidance offers short relief and longer uncertainty. Looking creates facts—and facts can become a plan.
Your first financial snapshot

pages.chapterTitle: Looking at Your Money Without Fear

rows: Do I know the household’s approximate monthly income?
Do I know our essential monthly expenses?
Do we have accessible emergency money?

pages.chapterTitle: Looking at Your Money Without Fear

rows: Do I understand our main insurance policies?
Could I manage essential payments for one week alone?

blocks.text: “Not sure” is not a failure. It is a clear next step. Replace “I do not know” with “I am finding out.”

pages.chapterTitle: Looking at Your Money Without Fear

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Confidence begins when you are willing to ask the question, not when you already know the answer.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one “not sure” item and locate only that information today.

pages.epigraph: “Life is unpredictable. Your first line of defence can still be planned.”

blocks.text: A medical test, job interruption, urgent journey or broken appliance becomes more stressful when the family must borrow immediately or sell a long-term investment.
What an emergency fund is—and is not

The target depends on essential expenses, income stability, number of earners, dependants, insurance and support. Start with the first achievable milestone rather than waiting for a perfect six-month number.

items: Keep emergency money accessible and easy to understand.
Do not expose the whole reserve to large market falls or long lock-ins.
Define who can access it and what counts as an emergency.
Refill it after use without blaming yourself.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: An emergency fund cannot prevent a difficult day. It can stop that day from becoming a long financial crisis.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Write your essential monthly expense and choose the first emergency-fund milestone you can build.

pages.epigraph: “A budget should help you do more of what matters—not make life feel smaller.”

blocks.text: Budgeting is often presented as a list of things to stop. A useful budget is a plan for what money should protect, provide and make possible.
A simple four-bucket plan

The proportions will differ by family and season. A newborn, career break or loan may temporarily change the plan. A budget has failed only when it no longer helps you make decisions—not when one month looks different.

blocks.label: JOY BELONGS IN THE PLAN
blocks.text: Rest, convenience and celebration have value. The goal is intentional spending, not punishment.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: A good budget reflects your family’s values rather than someone else’s percentages.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Look at the last three purchases. Would you happily choose each one again?

pages.chapterTitle: Giving Your Future Self a Gift
pages.epigraph: “Saving is one way of telling tomorrow: I remembered you.”

blocks.text: Mothers prepare for everyone: the next shoe size, the medicine box, the school form. Saving is the same skill directed toward the future—including your own.
Give savings a name

pages.chapterTitle: Giving Your Future Self a Gift
Giving Your Future Self a Gift

blocks.text: Naming a goal makes progress visible and helps protect the money from unrelated spending. Begin with an amount that can continue. Consistency is more valuable than an impressive first month followed by stress.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Saving is not only about having extra money. It is about creating extra choice.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Name one savings goal and arrange a small repeatable transfer or calendar reminder.

pages.epigraph: “Saving protects the near future. Investing may help prepare for a distant one.”

blocks.text: Saving and investing are close companions, not rivals. Saving prioritises access and stability. Investing accepts some uncertainty in pursuit of growth over time.

rows: Avoiding a large loss before the goal
Growth, inflation and the ability to stay invested

blocks.text: Inflation means a distant goal may cost more later. Investing can help, but it never removes risk. Begin with the goal and time horizon; never begin with the product everyone is discussing.

blocks.label: A GENTLE RULE
blocks.text: Do not invest money you may need for an emergency or near-term commitment in a product that can fall sharply or lock the money away.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Good investing is not chasing the fastest growth. It is choosing an appropriate risk for a meaningful goal.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Write one goal at least ten years away and the reason it matters.

pages.chapterTitle: Not Every Rupee Has the Same Job
pages.epigraph: “Money becomes easier to manage when its purpose is clear.”

blocks.text: Keeping salary, bills, emergency savings and long-term goals in one mental pile creates confusion. Separate the jobs before choosing the products.

pages.chapterTitle: Not Every Rupee Has the Same Job

rows: A clear date and suitable certainty

pages.chapterTitle: Not Every Rupee Has the Same Job

blocks.text: This separation can happen through accounts, sub-accounts, labels or simply a written plan. The aim is clarity, not opening unnecessary products.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Choose the purpose first. The right financial home is easier to evaluate afterward.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Draw four circles—Living, Safety, Dreams and Future—and place one goal in each.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing the Right Home for Your Money
pages.epigraph: “Different products exist because money has different jobs.”

blocks.text: A savings account, FD, government scheme, mutual fund and insurance policy are not competing answers to one question. Each is designed for a different purpose and trade-off.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing the Right Home for Your Money

rows: When will I need the money?
Determines how much lock-in or market movement may be acceptable.
Near-term obligations leave little room for loss.
How quickly must I access it?
Emergency and planned money have different liquidity needs.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing the Right Home for Your Money

rows: What could make me lose money?
Market, credit, inflation, fraud and penalty risks differ.
What will it cost after fees and tax?
The headline return is not what you necessarily keep.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing the Right Home for Your Money

blocks.label: DEPOSIT PROTECTION
blocks.text: Eligible deposits at an insured bank are covered by DICGC up to the prevailing limit per depositor per bank under applicable rules. This does not make every deposit choice identical or protect against inflation.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Do not ask “Which product gives the highest return?” before asking “What must this money do?”

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing the Right Home for Your Money

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one account or investment and write its job, date, access rules, risk, cost and nominee.

pages.epigraph: “You do not need to know every fund. You need to understand what the scheme is trying to do.”

blocks.text: A mutual fund pools money from investors and invests it according to a stated objective. An asset management company manages the portfolio; investors own units. Professional management and diversification can help, but neither guarantees profit.

rows: Higher short-term movement; used for suitable long horizons
Credit, interest-rate and liquidity risks remain
Risk depends on the actual allocation
Shares the index’s rises and falls
Not a bank deposit; small losses are possible

blocks.text: Read before investing

items: Scheme objective and portfolio category
Riskometer and time horizon
Expense ratio and other costs
Exit load and redemption process
Direct versus regular plan
Growth versus distribution option
Tax treatment for the current year

blocks.label: RED FLAGS
blocks.text: A fund is sold as guaranteed, “best,” risk-free or certain to repeat last year’s return; the seller ignores the riskometer or asks you to invest before you understand.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: A suitable fund is one that supports a goal at a risk you can understand and live with.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Read one scheme’s official objective and riskometer. Do not invest today—just translate them into plain language.

pages.epigraph: “A SIP is a method, not an investment and not a guarantee.”

blocks.text: A Systematic Investment Plan invests a chosen amount into a selected mutual-fund scheme at regular intervals. The scheme creates the risk and return; the SIP creates the schedule.
What a SIP can and cannot do

rows: Reduce the pressure to choose one perfect date
Buy more units when prices are lower and fewer when higher
Be reviewed and increased as circumstances improve

blocks.text: Choose an amount that can continue through ordinary months. Missing or pausing a SIP is not moral failure; it is a signal to review cash flow, the goal and whether the commitment remains realistic.

blocks.label: BEFORE STARTING
blocks.text: Confirm the goal, horizon, emergency reserve, scheme, riskometer, costs, bank mandate and nomination. A convenient auto-debit is still a financial decision.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Long-term wealth is usually built through ordinary decisions repeated—not dramatic predictions.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Calculate what a small monthly amount becomes in one year without assuming any investment return.

pages.epigraph: “Protection keeps one difficult event from undoing years of careful work.”

blocks.text: Financial protection rests on several pillars: emergency savings, health cover, life or accident cover where appropriate, and documents your family can locate.

rows: Can we manage a disruption without immediate debt?
What is covered, excluded, limited or subject to waiting?
Who depends financially on this person’s income or unpaid work?
What happens if work or caregiving ability changes?
Can a trusted adult locate the facts during a crisis?

blocks.text: Insurance is primarily a risk-transfer contract. Investment and insurance may be combined in some products, but that can make cost, surrender and return comparisons harder. Read the policy schedule, exclusions and benefit illustration.

blocks.label: FAMILY FINANCIAL FOLDER
blocks.text: Keep institution names, policy/account numbers, nominees, contacts and document locations. Do not store passwords, PINs, OTPs or CVVs in an ordinary folder.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Protection is not expecting the worst. It is reducing the financial burden if the unexpected arrives.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Locate one insurance schedule and write its cover, exclusions, renewal date, nominee and claim contact.

pages.epigraph: “A plan gives money direction—and gives the family a place to return when life changes.”

blocks.text: A financial plan answers three questions: Where are we today? Where do we want to go? What is the next realistic step?
Build the plan in order

rows: List income, expenses, assets, debts and existing protection.
Build accessible emergency money and review insurance.
Write the amount, date and reason for each goal.
Select products only after purpose, horizon and risk are clear.
Return annually and after major life changes.

blocks.text: Your one-page start
A plan is not a prediction. Income, children, careers, health and priorities change. A good plan changes with them without losing sight of the foundation.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Financial confidence is not having more than everyone else. It is knowing what your money is helping your family build.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose a Family Financial Review Day and put it in your calendar.

pages.epigraph: “A product is useful only when its job matches your goal.”

blocks.text: Meera had done something many families find difficult: she had saved ₹2,00,000. Then the advice began. One relative said “FD.” A colleague said “mutual fund.” A video said “gold.” Her bank mentioned insurance. The more options she heard, the less confident she felt.

Over tea, an older friend asked her four questions: When will you need the money? Must the amount remain stable? How quickly might you need access? What loss could you tolerate without panic? Meera did not receive a product recommendation that afternoon. She received something better—a way to think.
Start with the job, not the product

Every product makes a different trade-off among four things: safety of capital, access to money, potential growth and certainty of outcome. No product can maximise all four at once. If somebody claims otherwise, pause.

rows: A school fee due next year has a different job from retirement money needed in twenty years.
A shorter time horizon usually leaves less room to recover from market falls.
How quickly must I access it?
Liquidity matters for emergencies; a lock-in may be acceptable for a distant goal.
How much uncertainty can I accept?
Market-linked products can fall. “High return with no risk” is a warning, not a benefit.
What will it cost and how is it taxed?
Fees, penalties and taxes affect what you actually keep.

blocks.text: The product families
1. Bank deposits: stability and access

A savings account is designed for transactions and money you may need quickly. A fixed deposit (FD) holds a lump sum for a chosen term at an agreed rate, while a recurring deposit (RD) accepts regular deposits for a chosen term. These are deposits, not market investments.

Eligible deposits—including savings, fixed and recurring deposits—at an insured bank are covered by DICGC up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank, combining principal and interest and accounts held in the same right and capacity. Different branches of the same bank are aggregated; different banks receive separate limits.
Fact basis: Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation (DICGC) FAQs, checked 22 July 2026.

rows: Bills, near-term needs, part of emergency cash
Return may not keep pace with inflation; deposit-insurance limit applies per bank.
Chosen term; early closure rules vary
Reinvestment risk; penalty or lower interest on early closure; interest is generally taxable.
Building a regular saving habit for a known goal
Less flexible than a savings account; missed-instalment and closure rules vary.

blocks.label: REAL-LIFE EXAMPLE
blocks.text: A family expects a school admission payment in nine months. Their first concern may be access and capital stability—not maximising return. That does not make deposits “better”; it makes the product’s job a closer match to the goal.
2. Government small-savings products: rule-based, goal-specific

Products such as PPF, NSC, post-office time deposits and Sukanya Samriddhi accounts are governed by notified rules. They may offer sovereign backing and defined structures, but they are not interchangeable. Lock-ins, contribution limits, withdrawal rules and tax treatment differ. Chapter 17 explains the major schemes.
3. Bonds: you are lending money

When you buy a bond, you lend money to a government, company or other issuer. In return, the issuer may pay interest and repay the principal at maturity. A bond is not automatically “safe.” Its risks include the issuer failing to pay, market price changes when interest rates move, and difficulty selling before maturity.

blocks.label: ASK BEFORE BUYING A BOND
blocks.text: Who is the issuer? What is the credit rating—and what does the rating not guarantee? When does it mature? Can I sell it easily? Is the return fixed, floating or market-linked? What happens if the issuer defaults?
4. Mutual funds: pooled portfolios

A mutual fund pools money from investors and invests it according to a stated objective. Depending on the scheme, the portfolio may hold equities, debt securities, money-market instruments, gold or a mixture. Professional management and diversification can make investing more accessible, but neither removes risk.

rows: Values can fall sharply; sector or style concentration can add risk.
Credit defaults, interest-rate movements and liquidity problems can cause losses.
Risk depends on the actual allocation; “hybrid” does not mean guaranteed.
It will broadly share the index’s falls and may have tracking difference.
Not a bank deposit and not covered by DICGC; small losses are possible.

blocks.text: A SIP is only a method of investing a fixed amount regularly; it is not a separate product and it does not guarantee profit. Always read the scheme objective, riskometer, costs and exit-load rules.
Concept basis: SEBI Investor education pages on securities, mutual funds and asset classes, checked 22 July 2026.
5. Shares: ownership with real uncertainty

A share represents part-ownership of a company. Returns may come from dividends and a rise in the share price, but neither is assured. Company performance, valuation, industry conditions and the wider economy can all affect price. Direct shares require research, diversification and the ability to accept loss.

blocks.label: RED FLAGS
blocks.text: Guaranteed stock tips; pressure to act before a “secret” expires; unregistered advisers; promises to double money; requests to transfer money to a personal account; or anyone asking for your password, OTP or trading access.
6. ETFs: funds that trade on an exchange

An exchange-traded fund holds a portfolio—often tracking an index—but its units trade on a stock exchange like shares. It may offer diversification and lower ongoing costs, yet buying and selling can involve brokerage, bid–ask spreads and demat charges. Its market price can move during the day.
7. Gold: useful, but not magical

Gold can be held as jewellery, coins or bars, or through regulated financial products such as gold ETFs or eligible mutual funds. Jewellery includes making charges and may be poor for short-term resale. Physical gold needs secure storage and purity checks. Market-linked gold products still fluctuate. Gold produces no business earnings or interest merely because you hold it.
8. Real estate and REITs: property in two different forms

Direct property may offer use, rent or appreciation, but it usually needs a large amount, involves legal and maintenance work, and can take time to sell. A Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) gives investors exposure to income-generating real estate through listed units. REIT prices and distributions can change; they are not the same as owning a flat.
9. Insurance: protection first

Insurance transfers specified financial risks to an insurer under a contract. Health insurance helps with eligible medical costs; term life insurance protects dependants if the insured dies during the cover period. Investment-linked or savings-linked insurance combines functions and can be harder to compare. Read exclusions, waiting periods, surrender terms and benefit illustrations before signing.
One-page comparison

rows: Generally high, subject to bank and deposit-cover rules
When will I need the money?
Can I accept the lock-in and conditions?
Usually defined, not guaranteed against default
Does the scheme match my horizon and risk?
Can I research and diversify responsibly?
Low for property; market-linked for REITs
Can I handle concentration and liquidity?

blocks.text: Common mistakes

items: Choosing a product because a friend earned well from it.
Using emergency money for a long lock-in or volatile investment.
Comparing headline returns while ignoring tax, fees, inflation and risk.
Buying too many products and losing track of nominations, statements and goals.
Assuming “government-related,” “bank-sold” or “regulated” means “guaranteed profit.”
Signing forms without keeping a copy or checking what was actually purchased.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: The “best” product does not exist in isolation. A suitable product is one whose purpose, time horizon, access, risk, cost and tax treatment fit the job you have given your money.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one financial product you already own. Write down: its purpose, when you may need the money, how quickly you can access it, what could cause a loss, all charges, and the nomination status. If any answer is missing, your next step is to find it—not to buy something new.
Before you buy: a calm checklist

items: I can explain the product in two sentences.
I know whether returns are fixed, variable or merely illustrated.
I understand lock-in, exit and premature-closure rules.
I have checked risk, fees, tax treatment and nomination.
The intermediary is appropriately regulated or authorised.
I have read the official document, not only a message or sales slide.
I am not borrowing or using emergency money to invest.
I can say “no” and take time to decide.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know
pages.epigraph: “A scheme is an option to understand—not a box every family must tick.”

blocks.text: When Kavita opened a folder of family papers, she found a PPF passbook, an old insurance certificate, her daughter’s Sukanya account details and a note about NPS. She knew the names. She did not know what each one was for.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Her confusion was not carelessness. Government schemes are often discussed as if the label itself explains the product. It does not. The useful question is: What job was this scheme designed to do, who is eligible, and what rules make the money less flexible?

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: EDITION NOTE
blocks.text: This chapter describes broad rules verified from official portals on 22 July 2026. Small-savings interest rates are notified periodically; premiums, contribution rules, tax treatment and eligibility can change. Check the official portal before opening, renewing or claiming.
A map before the details

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: Adults seeking a long-horizon, rule-based account.
Long-term saving for an eligible girl child
Guardian of a girl under the prescribed opening age.
Eligible salaried employees in covered establishments.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: Eligible individuals seeking a regulated pension account.
Parents or guardians considering long-term retirement-oriented saving for a child.
Eligible non-income-taxpayers aged 18–40 with a bank/post-office savings account.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: People who understand the lock-in and current tax treatment.

blocks.text: Public Provident Fund (PPF)

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: PPF is a long-term individual savings account governed by the Public Provident Fund Scheme, 2019. Under the rules checked for this edition, an individual may open an account, and a guardian may open one for a minor. Joint accounts are not permitted. The annual contribution range is ₹500 to ₹1.5 lakh, with the ₹1.5 lakh limit including deposits in the person’s own account and eligible minor account.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: The original term is 15 years, with rule-based loans, partial withdrawals and extensions. The interest rate is notified by the Government and can change. PPF’s discipline can be useful for long-term saving; the same restrictions mean it is not a substitute for readily accessible emergency money.
Official basis: National Savings Institute, Public Provident Fund Scheme, 2019.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: Long-term personal savings, not short-term spending.
Restricted; loans/withdrawals are allowed only under scheme rules.
₹500 minimum and ₹1.5 lakh maximum per financial year under current rules.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: Commonly associated with old-regime section 80C treatment; verify current law and your regime.
Lock-in and changing notified rates; not market-price risk like equity.

blocks.text: Sukanya Samriddhi Account (SSA)

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: A guardian may open an SSA in the name of a girl child who has not attained age ten on the opening date. Usually, one account is allowed per girl and up to two girl children per family, with specified exceptions for multiple births. The account matures 21 years from opening. Deposits are required only for the period specified in the scheme, and education-related withdrawals are available under conditions.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: For the edition checked, annual deposits range from ₹250 to ₹1.5 lakh. This is a long-term, goal-specific account. It should not lead a family to neglect emergency savings, insurance, the parents’ retirement or the education needs of other children.
Official basis: National Savings Institute, Sukanya Samriddhi Account Scheme, 2019, as amended.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: REAL-LIFE EXAMPLE
blocks.text: A family can value SSA for its discipline and still use other tools for the same child’s education. One scheme does not have to carry the entire goal, especially when education costs and family circumstances change.
Employees’ Provident Fund (EPF), Pension Scheme and EDLI

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: EPF is employment-linked. Eligible employees in covered establishments contribute through payroll, with employer contributions allocated according to applicable rules. The wider EPFO framework also includes the Employees’ Pension Scheme (EPS) and Employees’ Deposit Linked Insurance (EDLI). Your Universal Account Number (UAN), nomination and employment records matter when you change jobs or make a claim.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

items: Check that your name, date of birth, PAN, Aadhaar and bank details match.
Review the passbook and employer deposits periodically.
Transfer or consolidate eligible balances when changing jobs instead of losing track of old member IDs.
Update e-nomination after marriage, childbirth or another major family change.
Do not treat an SMS or agent as the final authority; use EPFO’s official portal.

blocks.text: Official basis: Employees’ Provident Fund Organisation FAQs and member guidance.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: National Pension System (NPS)

NPS is a regulated, defined-contribution retirement system. Your outcome depends on contributions, investment performance, charges and exit rules; it is not a fixed guaranteed pension unless a separate guarantee is explicitly part of a scheme. Under the All Citizen Model guidance checked for this edition, eligible Indian citizens, NRIs and OCIs may open an account between ages 18 and 70, and may continue contributing up to age 85 under current PFRDA rules, subject to KYC and other conditions.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: NPS Tier I is the retirement account with withdrawal and exit restrictions. Tier II is a voluntary account available subject to rules and is not a substitute for Tier I. Choices may include active or automatic asset allocation across equity, corporate debt and government securities. Exit rules were updated recently, which is exactly why old articles should not be treated as permanent instructions.
Official basis: Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority, NPS All Citizen Model, updated January 2026.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: ASK BEFORE JOINING
blocks.text: What is my retirement goal? Which NPS account and investment choice am I selecting? What are the current exit, partial-withdrawal and annuity rules? What tax benefit, if any, applies under my chosen tax regime?
NPS Vatsalya

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: NPS Vatsalya permits a parent or legal guardian to open and manage an individual pension account for an eligible minor, who remains the sole beneficiary. After the child turns 18 and completes the required KYC, the account transitions under the applicable rules. It is retirement-oriented; it should not be mistaken for an education account with unrestricted access.
Official basis: PFRDA NPS Vatsalya Scheme Guidelines, 2025.
Atal Pension Yojana (APY)

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: APY is a voluntary pension scheme designed especially for people in the unorganised sector. An eligible Indian citizen must generally be aged 18 to 40 and hold a bank or post-office savings account. Since 1 October 2022, a person who is or has been an income-tax payer as of the application date cannot open a new APY account. Existing eligible subscribers covered by the transition rules may continue.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: The subscriber selects a guaranteed monthly pension slab—₹1,000 to ₹5,000—payable after age 60, and the contribution depends on joining age and chosen pension. “Guaranteed” refers to the scheme benefit under its rules, not unlimited growth or inflation protection.
Official basis: PFRDA Atal Pension Yojana portal and FAQs.
PMJJBY and PMSBY: simple protection, limited cover

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

rows: One-year renewable life cover for death due to any reason, subject to rules
Participating bank/post-office account holder, generally age 18–50 to join
Cover amount is limited; it may not replace adequate family life insurance.
One-year renewable accidental death and disability cover, subject to rules
Participating bank/post-office account holder, generally age 18–70
It is accident cover, not comprehensive life or health insurance.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Premiums are generally auto-debited with consent and can be revised. Check whether the cover is active, the correct account is linked, the nominee is updated and the claim process is known. Do not assume an old enrolment remains active without checking renewal and debit status.
Official basis: Jan Suraksha scheme rules for PMJJBY and PMSBY, checked 22 July 2026.
National Savings Certificate and Post Office Time Deposits

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: NSC and post-office time deposits are fixed-term small-savings products offered under notified rules. They can help with known-date goals when the saver accepts the term and access restrictions. Certain qualifying deposits may receive old-regime tax deductions, but interest taxation and deduction treatment must be checked for the relevant year and taxpayer.
Pradhan Mantri Jan-Dhan Yojana (PMJDY)

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.text: PMJDY supports access to basic banking services and may connect eligible account holders to benefits and social-security schemes. A basic account is not “only for receiving money”; it should be used carefully, with mobile details, nomination and KYC kept current. Eligibility for overdraft or linked benefits is subject to scheme and bank rules.
Common mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

items: Opening a scheme only for a tax deduction without understanding the lock-in.
Assuming every government scheme is suitable for every family.
Counting a child-focused account as the parents’ retirement plan.
Forgetting annual minimum contributions, auto-debits or nomination updates.
Relying on old interest rates, forwarded messages or an unofficial calculator.
Using the same goal money twice in a plan—for example, counting one PPF balance for both retirement and education.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: RED FLAGS
blocks.text: A person asks for cash or a transfer to a personal account; guarantees a special unofficial rate; asks for an OTP or password; sells a “government approved” product without an official scheme document; or says eligibility checks are unnecessary.
Scheme decision checklist

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

items: I am eligible under the current official rules.
I understand the scheme’s single main purpose.
I know the minimum, maximum, term and access conditions.
I checked the current rate or benefit on the official portal.
I understand tax treatment under my regime.
I know what happens if I miss a deposit, exit early or die.
Nominee or guardian details are correct.
The scheme fits my plan; it is not replacing an emergency fund or necessary insurance.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Government backing can reduce some risks, but it does not remove lock-in, inflation, eligibility, documentation or opportunity-cost questions. Understand the rulebook before you admire the label.

pages.chapterTitle: Government Schemes Every Indian Mother Should Know

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one government scheme your family already has. Find the latest official statement or passbook. Record the account number, purpose, owner, nominee, current balance, maturity or exit date, required contribution and official portal. Put the note in your family financial folder.

pages.epigraph: “Tax becomes less frightening when you separate income, deductions, tax paid and filing.”

blocks.text: Ananya returned to paid work from home after a four-year break. Her first client deducted tax before paying her. Her bank credited interest. She sold a mutual fund. Suddenly the words TDS, capital gains and ITR appeared in three different places.
She did not need to become a tax expert. She needed a map: What income did I earn? Which regime applies? What tax has already been paid? What must I report? When should I ask a professional?

blocks.label: IMPORTANT DATE NOTE
blocks.text: This chapter uses rules for income earned in financial year 2025–26 and returns filed for Assessment Year 2026–27, verified on 22 July 2026. From 1 April 2026, the Income-tax Act, 2025 introduced the term “Tax Year” for income earned in FY 2026–27 onward. Current-year rates, forms and definitions must always be rechecked.
The four-part tax map

rows: Money or value taxable under a recognised head
Salary records, bank interest, rent, business books, investment statements.
Exemptions and deductions allowed under the chosen regime
Form 16, proof of eligible payments, official tax rules.
TDS/TCS, advance tax and self-assessment tax
Your annual statement to the Income Tax Department
Correct ITR form, filed and verified on the official portal.

blocks.text: Income is wider than salary

For individuals, income is commonly organised under five heads: salary; house property; profits and gains from business or profession; capital gains; and income from other sources. A mother who earns from consulting, tuition, online sales or a home business may have business or professional income even if she does not call herself a business owner.

rows: Rent agreement, receipts, municipal tax and loan-interest documents.
Invoices, receipts, bank records, expense proofs, TDS certificates.
Sale of shares, funds, gold or property
Buy/sell statements, dates, costs, improvement and transfer expenses.

blocks.text: TDS is not a second tax

Tax Deducted at Source means the payer deducts tax and deposits it against your PAN. It is a credit toward your final tax liability, not always the final amount. If too much was deducted, the return may produce a refund; if too little was deducted, more may be payable.

Check Form 26AS and the Annual Information Statement (AIS). Compare them with your own records. An item appearing in AIS is a prompt to review, not a reason to blindly accept an error. Use the portal’s feedback process where appropriate.
New regime and old regime

For AY 2026–27, the new regime is the default for eligible individuals. It uses different slabs and allows fewer common exemptions and deductions. The old regime retains many deductions and exemptions but uses older slab rates. “New” does not automatically mean cheaper, and “old” does not automatically mean better. The comparison depends on income type, eligible deductions and personal circumstances.

rows: Must be chosen by opting out, subject to rules.
For most individuals under 60: ₹0–2.5L nil; 2.5–5L 5%; 5–10L 20%; above 10L 30%.
Up to ₹60,000 when total income does not exceed ₹12L, subject to law and exclusions
Up to ₹12,500 when total income does not exceed ₹5L, subject to law.
Many Chapter VI-A deductions are unavailable; permitted items must be checked
Common eligible deductions may include 80C and 80D, subject to limits and conditions.
Non-business cases can generally choose each year in the return; business/profession cases face special option rules
Business/profession taxpayers should obtain advice before switching because restrictions apply.

blocks.text: Official basis: Income Tax Department guidance for salaried individuals and ITR filing for AY 2026–27. Health and education cess is 4% on income tax plus surcharge, where applicable.

blocks.label: A CRUCIAL NUANCE
blocks.text: The section 87A rebate is not the same as a nil tax slab, and special-rate income—such as certain capital gains—may not receive the same rebate treatment. Do not assume “income up to ₹12 lakh means no tax in every situation.”
Deductions are not refunds

A deduction reduces taxable income; it does not give the full amount back. If an eligible ₹10,000 deduction reduces income taxed at 20%, the basic tax effect may be about ₹2,000 before cess—not ₹10,000. Buying an unsuitable product merely to “save tax” can cost more than the tax saved.

rows: What it may cover under applicable rules
Specified items such as eligible provident-fund contributions, PPF, life-insurance premium, NSC, tuition fees and qualifying principal repayment
Overall limit and conditions apply; generally not available in the default new regime.
Eligible health-insurance premium and specified preventive-health-check-up / medical expenditure rules
Limits vary by age and situation; payment-mode conditions apply.
Eligible personal or employer contributions under specified provisions
Availability and limits differ by regime and contribution type.
What it may cover under applicable rules
Treatment depends on self-occupied/let-out property and regime
Do not combine principal and interest rules.
Only eligible institutions and payment conditions qualify
Keep the required receipt and donation details.

blocks.text: A freelancer or home-business owner needs a system

Separate business and personal money as far as practical. Issue invoices, record receipts, preserve expense proofs, reconcile bank entries and set aside money for tax. Depending on income and activity, advance tax, GST, tax audit, books of account or presumptive taxation may become relevant. These thresholds and conditions change; seek a chartered accountant when the activity is no longer occasional or simple.

blocks.label: REAL-LIFE EXAMPLE
blocks.text: A home baker receives ₹80,000 in customer payments. That is revenue, not automatically profit. Eligible business expenses must be supported and classified correctly. Personal groceries do not become business expenses simply because both were bought at the same shop.
Capital gains: selling creates the tax event

When you sell shares, mutual funds, gold or property, tax may depend on the asset, purchase and sale dates, holding period, sale value, cost and special rules. A gain shown in an app may not equal the taxable gain. Keep contract notes and capital-gains statements, and seek help for property, foreign assets, complex trades or significant gains.
Which return form?

ITR-1 may suit many resident individuals with simple income within its eligibility conditions. ITR-2 is commonly relevant where there are capital gains but no business or professional income. ITR-3 covers business or professional income in many cases; ITR-4 may be available for eligible presumptive-income cases subject to conditions. Do not choose a form only because it looks shorter.

blocks.label: WHEN PROFESSIONAL HELP IS SENSIBLE
blocks.text: Business or professional income; property sale; foreign income or assets; unlisted shares; crypto or virtual digital assets; ESOPs; multiple properties; large or unusual capital gains; notices; losses to carry forward; inheritance questions; or uncertainty about the tax regime.
Your filing calendar

items: Every month: save invoices, investment statements and major receipts in one folder.
Every quarter: compare bank entries, TDS and income records; consider advance-tax obligations if relevant.
After year-end: collect Form 16/16A, interest certificates, capital-gains statements and deduction proofs.
Before filing: reconcile Form 26AS and AIS/TIS with your records; select the correct form and regime.
After filing: e-verify within the permitted time, save the acknowledgement and respond to notices only through verified channels.

blocks.text: Common mistakes

items: Assuming no salary means no tax return or no taxable income.
Ignoring bank interest, rent, freelance receipts or investment sales.
Treating TDS as proof that nothing more is due.
Choosing a regime without calculating both options.
Buying an investment only because 31 March is near.
Failing to e-verify the filed return.
Using an incorrect ITR form or missing foreign-asset disclosures.
Sharing portal passwords or OTPs with an unverified “refund agent.”

blocks.label: RED FLAGS
blocks.text: Messages promising an instant refund; links that imitate the tax portal; requests to pay tax into a personal UPI ID; threats demanding immediate action outside the official portal; or anyone asking for your password, OTP, card PIN or remote access to your phone.
A calm tax checklist

items: My PAN, Aadhaar and bank details are accurate and linked where required.
I listed every source of income, including interest and freelance receipts.
I compared Form 26AS and AIS/TIS with my records.
I calculated both regimes where I was eligible to choose.
I checked whether special-rate income changes the rebate result.
I selected the correct ITR form.
I paid any balance tax through the official channel.
I e-verified and saved the acknowledgement.
I know which documents to retain.

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Tax confidence is not memorising every section. It is knowing your income, keeping evidence, reconciling tax credits, choosing deliberately, filing correctly and asking for help before a small uncertainty becomes an expensive problem.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Create one folder named “Tax Year.” Add five subfolders: Income, Expenses, Investments, Tax Paid, and Returns. Download your latest Form 26AS and AIS from the official portal and compare the first page with your own records. Do not rush to correct anything until you understand the difference.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams
pages.epigraph: “A genuine opportunity can survive a pause. A scam usually cannot.”

blocks.text: Nisha was settling her baby when a caller said her bank account would be frozen within ten minutes. He knew her name and part of her card number. He sounded patient, official and helpful. All he needed, he said, was the OTP that had just arrived.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: Her heart began racing. Then she remembered one rule: a bank does not need her secret code to protect her account. She ended the call, opened the bank’s official app and contacted the number shown there. The account was fine. The emergency had been invented to make her act before she could think.
Scams do not begin with technology. They begin with pressure.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: Fraudsters commonly create fear, urgency, greed, affection, authority or embarrassment. The message may mention KYC expiry, a parcel, a police case, a refund, a job, a prize, an investment or a relative in trouble. The story changes; the pressure pattern is remarkably consistent.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

rows: “Act in ten minutes or your account will be blocked.”
End the contact. Verify through the official app, website or known number.
“I am calling from your bank, police, RBI or tax office.”
Do not rely on caller ID, uniform, logo or video. Verify independently.
“Do not tell your spouse or bank; the case is confidential.”
Speak to a trusted person. Legitimate processes do not require isolation.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

rows: “Pay a small fee to unlock a prize, job or profit.”
Do not pay to receive unexpected money.
“Send more money and we will recover your loss.”
Stop. Recovery scams often target people who have already been defrauded.

blocks.text: The secrets nobody should ask you to share

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

items: OTP, UPI PIN, ATM PIN, CVV, passwords or security answers.
A screen share, remote-access session or permission to control your phone.
A QR-code scan or UPI PIN entry to “receive” ordinary money.
Full card or account credentials through an unverified link or form.
Demat, trading or mutual-fund login credentials.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.label: THE SIMPLEST RULE
blocks.text: A PIN or OTP authorises access or payment. It is not needed to receive a normal refund, prize or incoming UPI transfer. If someone asks for it, end the interaction.
Official basis: RBI public-awareness guidance on payment frauds and safe digital payments, checked 22 July 2026.
Common scams and how they work
UPI collect requests and QR-code tricks

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: A fraudster says she is sending money, then sends a collect request or asks you to scan a QR code and enter your UPI PIN. You may actually be approving a debit. Read the entire payment screen. Do not approve a transaction you did not initiate and understand.
Fake KYC, bank and SIM calls

The caller warns that an account, card or mobile number will be blocked. A link installs a malicious app or captures credentials. Never use the link or number supplied in the threatening message. Contact the institution through its official channel.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: Remote-access and screen-sharing fraud

A supposed support agent asks you to install an app so she can “help.” Once granted access, the person may see OTPs, record the screen or operate apps. Banks do not need remote control of your personal phone to solve a routine complaint.
Task, part-time-job and work-from-home scams

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: The offer begins with easy tasks and may even pay a small amount to build trust. Then the victim must deposit money, buy a membership, recharge a wallet or pay “tax” to release earnings. Real work pays you; it does not repeatedly ask you to pay to unlock wages.
Investment groups, fake apps and finfluencer tips

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: A social-media group shows fabricated profits, celebrity videos or testimonials. Members are directed to an unknown app where a balance appears to grow, but withdrawals require further deposits. SEBI warns against guaranteed or near-certain returns, unregistered entities, complex unexplained strategies and pressure to act immediately.
Official basis: SEBI Investor “How to Spot a Scam,” cautions on Ponzi schemes and investor-awareness modules.
Loan and advance-fee scams

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: An app or caller promises instant credit but demands processing charges, insurance, a security deposit or access to contacts and photos. Verify that the lender and app are legitimate through official sources. Never borrow from an app simply because it appears in an advertisement or app store.
Impersonation, deepfakes and “family emergency” calls

A familiar photo, voice or video can be copied or generated. If a relative requests urgent money, call a known number or ask a private question. Agree on a family verification phrase that is never posted online.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: Digital arrest and authority impersonation

A caller claims you are under investigation and must remain on video, transfer money for “verification” or keep the matter secret. Do not transfer money. End the call and verify through official law-enforcement channels. Fear is being used as a payment tool.
A two-minute verification routine

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

items: Pause. Put the phone down or step away from the message.
Verify the person or institution using a contact you find independently.
Read the payment screen: payee, amount and whether money is being sent or requested.
Check registration where relevant—such as a SEBI-regulated intermediary.
Ask a trusted person before sending money under pressure.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.label: RED FLAGS
blocks.text: Guaranteed high returns; daily profits; “limited slots”; secret tips; requests for cash or a personal account; spelling or domain changes; unofficial apps; a demand to stay on a call; a request to borrow from someone else; or repeated fees needed to release your own money.
If money has already left: act now

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

rows: Contact your bank, card issuer, wallet or payment app through its official emergency channel. Ask to block the instrument/account and raise a fraud dispute.
Rapid reporting may help prevent further transactions and supports the investigation.
Call India’s National Cybercrime Helpline 1930 and report at cybercrime.gov.in. Keep the acknowledgement number.
The official reporting system coordinates with financial intermediaries; speed matters.
Preserve evidence: screenshots, phone numbers, URLs, UPI IDs, transaction IDs, messages, emails and call times.
Do not delete the trail, even if it feels embarrassing.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

rows: Change affected passwords from a clean device; remove remote-access apps; contact your telecom provider if SIM compromise is suspected.
Monitor accounts and credit information. Follow the bank’s written complaint and escalation process.
A fraud can lead to later attempts or identity misuse.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.text: Official reporting: National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal and helpline 1930, checked 22 July 2026.

blocks.label: DO NOT LET SHAME DELAY YOU
blocks.text: Intelligent, careful people are defrauded because scams are designed to manipulate ordinary human reactions. Reporting quickly is a protective action, not an admission of foolishness.
Build a safer family system

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

items: Use unique passwords and enable strong authentication where available.
Set transaction alerts and sensible daily limits.
Keep banking and investment apps updated; install apps only from verified publishers.
Review bank, card, demat and investment statements.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

items: Limit personal information visible on social media.
Teach children and older relatives the family rule: no money transfer during an unexpected call without independent verification.
Store official emergency numbers separately; do not rely on numbers inside suspicious messages.

blocks.text: Common mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

items: Staying on the call while trying to verify the caller.
Searching for a customer-care number and trusting the first advertisement.
Assuming a blue tick, logo, caller ID or video proves identity.
Sending a small “test” payment to an unknown person.
Paying again to recover earlier money.
Deleting messages before saving evidence.
Waiting until the next day because the amount seems small.

pages.chapterTitle: Staying Safe from Frauds and Scams

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Your strongest protection is not technical brilliance. It is permission to stop. End the call, close the link, verify independently and speak to someone you trust.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Save your bank’s official fraud-reporting number, 1930 and cybercrime.gov.in in your family financial folder. Then agree on a private family verification phrase for urgent money requests.

pages.epigraph: “You do not need to memorise the language. You need to know what question each word is answering.”

blocks.text: The first time Farah met a financial adviser, the conversation moved quickly: inflation, liquidity, NAV, CAGR, nominee, term cover and tax deduction. She understood each sentence separately, yet left feeling she had missed something important.
The problem was not her ability. Finance often compresses a full idea into one small word. This chapter opens those words again. Use it as a reference, not an exam.

blocks.label: HOW TO USE THIS CHAPTER
blocks.text: When you meet an unfamiliar term, read the plain meaning and then ask the suggested question. Understanding is more useful than repeating a definition.
Everyday money and planning

rows: Something you own that has financial value.
How easily can it be valued and sold?
Money you owe or a financial obligation.
What is the interest rate and repayment date?
Assets minus liabilities—a snapshot, not a judgement.
Is it moving in the right direction over time?
Money coming in and going out during a period.
Do regular outflows leave room for goals?
A plan for how money will be used.
Does it reflect what matters to our family?
Accessible money reserved for genuine disruptions.
How many months of essentials can it cover?
A general rise in prices that reduces purchasing power.
Will this goal cost more by the time I need it?
How quickly an asset can become usable cash without a large loss.
Can I access it when the goal arrives?
How long until you expect to need the money.
Can the investment recover from short-term falls?

blocks.text: Saving, returns and risk

rows: The original amount deposited, borrowed or invested.
Price paid for borrowing money, or earned on certain savings/lending products.
A guaranteed real return after inflation and tax.
Interest calculated only on the principal.
Returns can earn further returns over time; losses can compound too.
Gain or loss over a period, often shown as a percentage.
Cash in hand after tax and charges.
Total percentage change between start and end.
Smoothed annual growth rate over multiple years; actual yearly returns may vary.
Possibility that the outcome differs from what you need, including loss.
How sharply and frequently a price moves.
Permanent loss; they are related but not identical.
Spreading exposure so one holding does not decide the whole result.
Owning many versions of the same risk.
How money is divided among asset classes such as equity, debt, cash and gold.
Emotional and financial ability to accept uncertainty.
Risk capacity—the loss your plan can actually bear.

blocks.text: Banking and borrowing

rows: Deposit account for transactions and accessible money.
Minimum balance, fees, interest and fraud controls.
A lump sum placed for a chosen term at an agreed rate.
Premature closure, tax and reinvestment risk.
Regular deposits for a chosen term.
Regular repayment containing principal and interest.
A low EMI caused only by a very long tenure.
A bureau’s summary indicator of credit behaviour.
Errors or identity misuse; check the report, not only the score.
The lender may enforce rights over the asset after default.
Interest calculated on outstanding loan principal.
A flat rate quoted in a way that looks cheaper.
Broader annual borrowing cost where disclosed.
A headline interest rate that excludes charges.
Temporary permitted pause or reduction in payment.

blocks.text: Mutual funds and securities

rows: Can I accept a substantial fall?
Money lent to an issuer under stated terms.
Who owes me, and can they repay?
Pooled portfolio managed to a stated objective.
What does the scheme own and what is its riskometer?
Per-unit value of a mutual fund’s net assets.
Am I wrongly treating a low NAV as “cheap”?
Method of investing a chosen amount regularly.
Can I continue comfortably through market falls?
Recurring fund operating cost charged to the scheme.
What do I receive for the cost?
Charge that may apply when redeeming within a stated period.
Do I have the knowledge or regulated advice needed?
Plan bought through a distributor, with distribution cost in expenses.
What service am I receiving and paying for?
Returns remain invested in the scheme.
Does it match my need for accumulation?
Distributions subject to availability and rules; not guaranteed interest.
Am I mistaking my own investment value for “extra” income?
Fund designed to track a stated index.
What index, cost and tracking difference?
Fund units traded on an exchange.
What are brokerage, spread, liquidity and demat costs?
Market value of a company’s equity.
How concentrated is my exposure by company size?

blocks.text: Insurance and family protection

rows: Frequency, due date and consequences of non-payment.
Maximum cover under specified policy terms.
Life cover for a period, primarily for protection.
Claim conditions, disclosures, nominee and adequate cover.
Time during which specified claims are not covered.
Which condition has which waiting period?
Event or condition the policy does not cover.
Read the wording, not only the brochure.
Share of an eligible claim paid by the insured.
Whether it applies by age, treatment or hospital.
Amount borne before the insurer pays under the policy terms.
Eligible treatment settled with a network provider under approval rules.
Cashless does not mean every cost is covered.
Person named to receive or handle proceeds under applicable rules.
Nomination may not always settle legal ownership.
Legal document stating how an estate should be handled after death.
Execution, witnesses and current legal advice.

blocks.text: Tax and retirement

rows: Income before permitted deductions and adjustments.
It is not always taxable income.
Income on which tax is computed after applicable rules.
Special-rate income may be treated differently.
It is not a rupee-for-rupee refund.
Income or allowance excluded under specified conditions.
Reduction from calculated tax subject to conditions.
It is not the same as a nil slab.
Tax deducted by payer and credited against PAN.
It may be more or less than final liability.
Taxable gain determined when an asset is transferred under the rules.
App profit is not necessarily taxable gain.
Periodic retirement income under scheme/contract rules.
Whether it is guaranteed, market-linked or inflation-adjusted.
Contract that converts money into periodic payments.
Rate, options, tax and what happens after death.
Point when specified benefit or value becomes available under rules.
Access conditions and tax can differ.
Accumulated pool of money for a goal.
A large number is not meaningful without the goal and time.

blocks.text: Words that deserve an immediate follow-up question

rows: Guaranteed by whom, under which written clause, and what risks remain?
Safe from market loss, default, fraud, inflation or lack of access?
Which part is tax-free, under which law and tax regime, and at what stage?
Are there embedded commissions, spreads, penalties or opportunity costs?
Over what period, before or after fees and tax, and with what maximum fall?
Which regulator or notification, and does approval guarantee return?
Best for which goal, horizon, liquidity need and risk capacity?

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: Financial language should make a decision clearer. If a term is being used to hurry, impress or silence you, ask for a plain-language explanation and written evidence.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Circle five terms you have seen in your own statements or policies. Explain each one aloud without looking. Wherever you hesitate, write one question to ask the bank, insurer, adviser or official helpline.

pages.epigraph: “A good financial system should make life calmer, not give you another subject to feel guilty about.”

blocks.text: Leela used to keep financial tasks in her head: renew the insurance, check the school fund, find the old FD, update the nominee. Each task was small, but together they created a quiet background worry.

Then she chose one Sunday every year as her Family Financial Review Day. She did not solve everything. She gathered the facts, chose three priorities and put the next dates in her calendar. This planner is designed to help you do the same.

blocks.label: HOW TO USE THESE PAGES
blocks.text: Complete what you know. Write “to find” where information is missing. Do not place passwords, PINs, OTPs or full card security details in this planner. Store the finished copy securely and tell one trusted adult where it is kept.
1. My financial confidence snapshot

rows: I know our regular monthly income and essential expenses.
We have an accessible emergency fund.
I understand our health and life cover.
I know our loans, rates and repayment dates.
Our nominees and important documents are current.
Every investment has a named goal.
I can locate tax records and recent returns.
I know what to do after a suspected financial fraud.

blocks.text: 2. Monthly money map

blocks.label: REFLECTION
blocks.text: Which three expenses protect or improve family life most? Which two outflows no longer feel worth their cost? What irregular annual expenses should become monthly sinking funds?
3. Emergency-fund worksheet
Target months of cover: __________ × Essential monthly expense ₹ __________ = Target ₹ __________
Current accessible emergency money: ₹ ____________________

blocks.label: CHECK THE JOB
blocks.text: Emergency money should be accessible and understandable. Record where it is held, who can access it and what counts as an emergency in your family.
4. Goals: give every rupee one job

blocks.label: PRIORITY CHECK
blocks.text: A goal can be important without being first. Protect the foundation—cash flow, emergency savings and suitable insurance—before stretching the family budget for distant dreams.
5. Debt map
The debt I want to understand or reduce first is: ______________________________________________
6. Insurance inventory

items: Read the policy schedule and exclusions, not only the premium receipt.
Check whether employer cover would continue after a job change.
Record claim contacts and the physical/digital location of documents.
Review cover after childbirth, a home loan, income change or new dependant.

blocks.text: 7. Investment inventory

blocks.label: QUALITY CHECK
blocks.text: Can you explain why each investment exists, what could make it lose value, when the money is needed, all costs and how to exit? If not, mark it “understand before adding more.”
8. Family financial folder

rows: PAN, tax returns, Form 16/16A and tax records

blocks.label: SECURITY NOTE

blocks.text: A planner is not proof that you have everything under control. It is a place to return when life changes. Progress may look like one updated nominee, one honest conversation or one account finally understood.
